analysis/index.title: Analysis
analysis/index.content:
Analysis is where you study those things that are continuous. you take limits. you can go to infinity. You study functions, measures, and operators.
Microlocal Analysis
Analytic Number Theory

analysis/microlocal-analysis.title: Microlocal Analysis
analysis/microlocal-analysis.content:
Microlocal Analysis is a a branch of Analysis performed on a cotangent bundle, or rather, phase space. Microlocal analysts act like they can take the Fourier transform anywhere. They get away with this by defining various cutoff functions to localize a function at a single point (x,\xi) in phase space.
It is analysis using both position and momentum.
Microlocal Sheaf
The greatest part of Microlocal Analysis on a Lie Manifold is that you can relate differential operators into algebraic symbols!
Fourier multiple! \Delta= F
But the Fourier transform is global, pulls far away things back to you! So localizing removes everything outside.
Why you use elliptic reuglarity and cut offs.
Resolvent
poisson bracket
pseudo-differential operator

how is the program structured?
phases of the PhD program

Core Phase: master a common core of fundamental mathematics, qualifying exams <3
specialization phase: advanced coursework, research experience, indepenetned study, oral exam (can you read literature?). Reach the boundary of current mathematics…
Disseratation phase: push the boundary further out!

types of funding

TA: variety of teaching, co-teaching, assistant.
first year: you will be assisting in the class of an instructor. be in the class, helping facilitate active learning, group work, but also helping with grading. office hours. lectures twice during the semester.
Graduate fellowship. separate letter. sometimes supplements a half TA.
expect to be funded minimally, health insurance, and full tuition.
graduate access fellowships. first year students
Additional funding sources. RA, outreach scholar, universitty felow, robin, likins, harvill, grogan


Amount of funding: $25,654 for 9 months

in other 3 months, you can do something else. a course you can teach over the summer. or do an internship.
cost of rent: $700-900/month for shared.


Total of 5-6 years with satisfactory progress!

lattices/analysis-2-review.title: Analysis 2 review
lattices/analysis-2-review.content:
5-6 questions, two hours
X metric space

A collection of subsets of X, \mathcal{A}, is an Algebra if
1. \varnothing, X\in \mathcal{A}, it is closed under complementation, and it is closed under intersection

σ-algebra: algebra that is closed under countable intersection (and therefore countable union)
X, $\mathbb


In \mathbb{R}, Borel σ-algebra is generated by open intervals
Measure on X is a triple, (X, A, μ), A is σ-algebra.

μ(\varnothing)=0
countable additivity, if A_i\in A disjoint, \mu (\bigcup_i A_i)=\sum_i \mu(A_i)


Properties of  a measure

monotonicity: A\subset B \Rightarrow \mu(A)\leq\mu(B)
countable sub-additivity: for any A_i\inA countable, \mu (\bigcup_i A_i)\leq\sum_i \mu(A_i)
if A_i\inA such that A_i\uparrowA, A_1\subset A_2\subset..., $\lim
If A


Construction of Measures

Outer measure \mu^*\rightarrow A_{\mu^*}=\{\mu^*\text{-measureable subsets}\}

A is \mu^*-measureable if \forall E\subset X, \mu^*(E)=\mu^*(E\cap A)+\mu^*(E\cap A^c)
\mu^*:\mathbb{P}(X)\rightarrow[0,\infty]
A\subset B\Rightarrow \mu^*(A)\leq\mu^*(B)
countable subadditivity


\mu=\mu^*|_{A_{\mu^*}} is a measure


If C\subset\mathbb{P}(X) and l:C\to[0,\infty] with \varnothing, X \in C, then \mu^*(E)=\inf\{\sum L(C_i)| E\subset \bigcup_i C_i, C_i\in C\} is an outer measure
The Lebesgue outer measure for E\subset\mathbb{R}: m^*(E)=\inf\{\sum_i l(I_i)| E\subset \bigcup_i I_i, I_i \text{finite open intervals}\}

\mathcal{M}={Lebesgue measureable subsets}, m= Lebesgue measure


Properties of Lebesgue Measure

Intervals are Lebesgue m’ble, and hence \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\subset\mathcal{M}

m(I)=length(I)


m = the unique translation-invariant measure on \mathcal{M} such that m[0,1]=1

m unique measure on \mathcal{M} such that m(a,b)=b-a \forall a<b


Approximation property for A\in\mathcal{M}

\forall \epsilon>0, there exists F closed and G open such that F\subset A\subset G such that m(G-F)<ε
F can be countable union of closed sets. G can be countable intersection of open sets such that m(G-F)=0.
Any lebesgue measureable set is union of borel set an
d null set




There exists a non lebesgue measureable subset of \mathbb{R}. (e.g. Vitali set)

In any A\in \mathcal{M} with m(A)>0, A  contains a non lebesgue measureable subset


There exists a lebesge measureable subset which is not Borel (used Cantor function/Devils staircase function)
Lebesgue measuerable functions f:A\to\mathbb{R}, A\in\mathcal{M}

f is lebesgue measureable if f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}) is measureable for all open \mathcal{O}\subset\mathbb{R}


Properties of lebesuge measureable function f, g

max(f^k*g+100f,-5g) is measureabe
However, composition of two measureable functions is not measureable in general, only have f\circ g is measureable if f is continuous

(f\circ g)^{-1}(\mathcal{O})=g^{-1}(f^{-1}(\mathcal{O})) is open for all open \mathcal{O}


Examples

continuous functions
any measureable function is a pointwise limit of a sequnece of ISFs
Integrable Simple Functions: s=\sum_{i=1}^ka_i1_{E_i}, E_i disjoint measurable m(E_i)\leq\infty.




Lebesgue Integral

ISF s, \int s=\sum a_im(E_i)
Non-negative m’ble function f, \int f=\sup\{\int s| 0\leq s\leq f, s \text{ISF}\}
f m’ble function f:A\to\mathbb{R}, f is integrable if \int|f|1_A < \infty, and in that case \int_A f = \int _A f^+ - \int_A f^-

|f|=f^+ + f^-, f^+=\max\{f,0\}, f^-=\min\{-f,0\}

lattices/alg-number-theory-midterm.title: alg number theory midterm
lattices/alg-number-theory-midterm.content:
Algebraic numbers/integers
number fields and subring of algebraic integers
algebraic integers are eigenvalues of integral matrices/lattice transformations
For a degree d field extensions F/Q, O_F\simeq Z^d as an additive group.
For L/K finite extension of fields, tr_{L/K}:L\to K, nm_{L/K}:L\to K.
Extension of degree d has an additive basis of degree d. Eg Q[t]/f(t) has basis \{1,t,...,t^{d-1}\}.
Trace is addidtive, Norm is mulitplicative!
If L/K is a fintie separable field extension, trace pairing non-degenerate ( for a nonzero v, there exists w such that Tr(vw)\neq0)
If A/k is a separable k-algebra, then A\otimes\bar k\simeq\prod_{i=1}^n \bar k . where n=\dim_k A
an extension being separable means rootsof a mininmal polynomial that splits are distinct
If K/k is a separable extension of degree n, then there exist n distinct embeddings of K\to\bar k
A separation is Galois if every embedding i:K\to \bar k has the same image.
K/k Galois, then |\text{Gal}(K/k)|=[K:k]
separability is like diagonalizability of the multiplication matrix acting on K\otimes_k\bar k\simeq \prod \bar k
If K/k is separable, trace of an element the sum of embeddings, norm is the product of embeddings
Variant: if K/k is aGalois, tr(\omega)=\sum_{\sigma\in \text{Gal}(K/k)}\sigma(\omega), norm is similarly product of conjguates
—
Factorization in O_F breaks down! so we want to consider factorization of ideals into prime ideals…3

_finnley/phd-requirements.title: PhD Requirements
_finnley/phd-requirements.content:
36 units of graduate credits in the major and 9-12 in a supporting minor.

18 units of dissertation (MATH 920) must be completed.
Cannot register for dissertation until I have passed oral exams.


Traditional Core Courses

Algebra, Real Analysis, and Geometry-Topology.
Need a B or better in both semesters or a high pass on the corresponding qualifying exam.


Further Mathematics Coursework

Two year-long mathematics course sequences not co-convened or apart of core requirements.
consider taking Complex Analysis (MATH 520 A-B).


Encouraged to take out-of-department courses or internships. They may be applied towards course units or professional development requirements.
PhD Minor

can be declared in mathematics or in a supporting discipline.


Plan of Study

completed after passing quals.
must present coherent collection of course in which the work outside of Mathematics is related to part of the studies in mathematics.



B. Graduate Faculty Advisor
First year, select an advisor and have it approvaed by the director of graduate studies (DGS). Their responsibilities are to:

be a source of academic information
provide assitstance in determining the plan of study
be proficient with forms in GradPath
meet periodically, provide timel input.

C. Research Tutorial Groups
enroll in MATH 596G spriing of first year. One course unit in whcih faculty members present short lecture series on resarch tpics of current interest. In the following fall, students choose one of the proposed topics and work with the correspodning faculty member on a reserach proejct.
D. Qualifying Examination

written exam in algebra
written exam in analysis
written exam in geometry and topology
an MS thesis

fail, pass, or high pass.
the written exams cover mateiral from the core courses.
E. Comprehensive Exam

write a short apaper whichh may give an account of a resarch problem of interest, signifcant example, or signifcant computations.


updated CV


oral examination

F. Prospectus

detailed plan for last years of program

Professional Development

required yearly after year 1.
internship, grad college workshops, interview current and/or graduated students and/or postdocs
giving a talk
etc

_finnley/undergraduate-review.title: Undergraduate Review
_finnley/undergraduate-review.content:
As listed as preliminary in the math grad handbook.
A. Linear Algebra
Essential Requirements

Gaussian elimination (“row reduction”) and applications to systems of equations, ranks.
Vector spaces and linear transformations, subspaces, basis, replacement theorem. Kernel and
image of a linear transformation.
Basis of a vector space. The matrix of a linear transformation with respect to two bases. Change
of basis relation to similar matrices.
Determinants, traces, characteristic polynomial.
Eigenvectors and eigenvalues. Canonical forms including Jordan form. Minimal polynomial. Two
criterion of diagonalizability (1) dimension of eigenspaces = algebraic multiplicity (2) minimal
polynomial has no repeated roots.
Euclidean spaces. Orthonormal bases and the Gram-Schmidt process, relation with QR
decomposition. Orthogonal complement, orthogonal projection.
Self-adjoint operators and symmetric matrices. Spectral theorems, Singular Value
Decomposition.

More advanced topics

Row rank equals column rank and relation to maximal invertible square submatrix
Direct sums, quotient spaces, fundamental theorem of linear operators: the image of a linear map
f is isomorphic to the quotient of the domain of f by the kernel of f. The dual space and the
transpose of a linear transformation.
Equivalence of the following (1) finding a basis such that the matrix representing a linear
transform is in a simple form (2) canonical form of a matrix.
Vandermonde determinant. The determinant, trace, and characteristic polynomial in terms of the
eigenvalues. Cayley-Hamilton Theorem.
Decompose space into direct sum of generalized eigenspaces. Nilpotent operator. Simultaneous
diagonalization.
Hermitian spaces, Orthonormal Basis, decomposition of any matrix as a product of Hermitian and
unitary. Bilinear forms, canonical form of quadratic and skew-symmetric bilinear forms,
signatures.
Positive definite symmetric matrices. Normal operators, unitary operators. Rayleigh quotient.

B. Vector Calculus and Differential Equations
Essential requirements

The derivative of a function 𝑓𝑓: 𝑅𝑅𝑛𝑛 → 𝑅𝑅𝑛𝑛. Linear approximation.
Line integrals. The multidimensional Riemann integral. Change of variables formula.
Div, grad, curl and relations between them. Divergence theorem, Stokes’ theorem, Green’s
theorem.
Existence and uniqueness of solutions to a first order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODE).
First and second order linear ODEs with constant coefficients.
Higher order linear ODEs and systems of first order ODEs.

More advanced topics
7. Implicit and inverse function theorem
8. Multivariate Taylor series
9. Existence and Uniqueness theorem of systems of ODE
C. Complex Analysis
Essential requirements

Holomorphic (analytic) functions.
Elementary functions and the mappings that they define.
Conformal mappings. Problems such as finding a conformal mapping from a quadrant to a disc.
The Taylor series of a holomorphic function.
Contour integrals. Cauchy’s theorem. The Cauchy formula.
Poles. Meromorphic functions. Laurent series. Residues.
Evaluating contour integrals of meromorphic functions.
Using complex integration for evaluating some improper integrals.

D. Abstract Algebra
Essential requirements

Basic examples of groups: cyclic groups, dihedral groups, permutation groups, alternating groups, and matrix groups.
Subgroups, homomorphisms, and isomorphisms.
Cosets, quotients, Lagrange’s theorem, and the first isomorphism theorem.
Rings, ideals, and quotient rings.
Integral domains, prime ideals and maximal ideals.
Principal ideals and principal ideal domains (PIDs). Examples such as Z and F[x].
Unique factorization in PIDs.
Finite and algebraic extensions, splitting fields, and finite fields.

More advanced topics
9. Structure of finitely generated abelian groups.
10. Group actions on sets. Orbits–stabilizers theorem and applications.
11. Simple groups and the Sylow theorems.
12. Geometric constructions of fields and Galois Theory
13. Galois theory and solvability by radicals.
E. Real Analysis
Essential Requirements

Limits and continuity, including application of the 𝜀𝜀 − 𝛿𝛿 definition.
Integrals, including improper integrals. Convergence tests.
Sequences and series. Taylor series. Radius of convergence.
Series of functions. Uniform convergence.
Exchanging the order of summation and limit.
Differentiation and integration of series of functions.
Countable and uncountable sets. Axiom of choice and Zorn’s lemma.
Axioms for R.

More advanced topics
9. Lebesgue integral
F. Point Set Topology

Basics of set theory, inclusion/subset, injection, surjection, bijection.
Open, closed, connected, and compact subsets of \R^n. Heine-Borel theorem.
Topological spaces and Hausdorff spaces.
Metric spaces as topological spaces.
Continuous functions.
Compact sets. Images of compact sets are compact. Continuous functions achieve their maximum on compact sets.
Connected sets. Images of connected sets are connected.

Advanced topics:
8. Tychonoff Theorem,
9. Metricization
10. Fundamental group.

_finnley/old/lattice-visibility-thesis-i.title: Lattice Visibility Thesis Idea
_finnley/old/lattice-visibility-thesis-i.content:
Given a sublattice \Lambda\subset\Z^n, what is the density of visible points on the sublattice?
Here I’m defining a sublattice to be the span of m linearly independent vectors in \Z^n, m\leq n.
For some fixed x\in\Z^n, a lattice point y is said to be visible from x if and only if \gcd(y-x)=1.
Visibility of \Lambda from x = Density of points in \Lambda that are visible from x

can be defined asymptotically. Let N(r)=\{y\in\Lambda : ||y||_{\infty} \leq r\} and V(r)=\{y\in\Lambda : ||y||_{\infty} \leq r, \gcd(y-x)=1\}. The density is defined to be \lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{|V(r)|}{|N(r)|}.

Classical Result: Visibility of \Z^n from any point in \Z^n is \frac{1}{\zeta(n)}, where \zeta
is the Riemann-Zeta function.
Goals
Visibility of \Z^m\times\{0\}^{n-m}\subset\Z^n from any point x\in\Z^n.
Visibility of lattice generated by unimodular nxm matrix M from any point x\in\Z^n
- I think I have this for m=n-1… old hyperplane result
- i think this result should follow from the previous… Just transform the basis of the \Z^m\times\{0\}^{n-m} by the matrix, which preserves visibility
Visibility of \Lambda=p\Z\times\Z for p prime and any point x\in\Lambda
Visibility of \Lambda =p\Z^m\times\Z^{n-m}
Visibility of p_{1}\Z\times p_{2}\Z for p_1, p_2 distinct primes
p_{1}\Z^{m_{1}}\times p_{2}\Z^{m_{2}}
p_{1}\Z^{m_{1}}\times p_{2}\Z^{m_{2}}\times\Z^{m_{3}}
Visibility of a_{1}\Z\times a_{2}\Z for a_{1},a_{2} coprime
a_{1}\Z^{m_{1}}\times a_{2}\Z^{m_{2}}
a_{1}\Z^{m_{1}}\times a_{2}\Z^{m_{2}}\times\Z^{m_{3}}
Visibility of \Lambda = \Pi_{i=1}^r a_i\Z^{m_i} where \gcd_i(a_i)=1

from any intrinsic point x\in\Lambda.
from any extrinsic point x\in\Z^n

If D=<a_1,...,a_n> diagonal matrix and U, V\in \SL_{n}(\Z), what is density of UDV?
I mean, it should be the same

_finnley/old/outline.title: Outline
_finnley/old/outline.content:
0.  Preliminaries
0.1. Natural Density
What sorts of sets can I consider natural density on?
According to arXiv:1803.06481v1 The density of visible points in the Amman-Beenker point set by Gustav Hammarhjelm, September 23, 2018:

Locally finite point sets \mathcal{P}\subset\mathbb{R}^d has an asymptotic density if \lim_{R\to\infty}\frac{|(\mathcal{P}\cap RD)|}{vol(RD)}=\theta(\mathcal{P}) holds for all Jordan measurable D\subset\mathbb{R}^d
Peano-Jordan Measure, also called the Jordan content since it isn’t technically a measure in the modern sense!

But it looks like it’s exactly what I need. defined on half open intervals, I can define the measure on the fundamental tile/region/domain of each lattice! its kinda perfect actually
Jordan content and Natural Asymptotic Density
Involves a lower and upper bound… literally exactly what Kelly and I have been trying to do!
mathworld.wolfram.com/JordanMeasure.html
I need to come up with a rigorous definition of the density of visible lattice points on any lattice.
0.2. What is a Lattice?

A finitely generated free abelian group

doc.sagemath.org/html/en/reference/modules/sage/modules/free_quadratic_module_integer_symmetric.html
doc.sagemath.org/html/en/reference/quadratic_forms/index.html
equipped with a non degenerate bilinear form LxL\to\Z?
ambient quadratic space Q


A subspace of \mathbb{Z}^n
Identifiable with some sort of quotient space? Toroidal space?

Chinese Remainder Theorem


Cosets x+\Lambda for some x\in\mathbb{Z}^n?
Affine subspaces, hyperplanes, solutions to systems of linear equations
Crystallography!
Tiling Theory!

I think I need to only consider lattices that are proper SUBGROUPS of \Z^n, meaning they must pass through the origin. Contain inverses (easy). Closed under vector addition and subtraction (easy). Identity (easy). whatever
I think the “visibility from a point x of a set = visibility from the origin a set translated by x” lemma will serve as my workaround for considering translated lattices. Plus these then serve as the cosets of sublattice! for all x\in\Z
Lattice terminology:

m-dimensional lattice
Matrix generting a lattice, basis of a lattice
fundamental parallelotope/domain/region

Gramian and m-dimensional volume of fundamental tile

discriminant
even lattices
root lattices

aeb.win.tue.nl/latt/lattices.pdf this is useful source
I think it’s important for me to categorize what a lattice is early on. This will make apparent the connections I’m drawing between so many fields of math, simply by how many ways we can view lattices!
Omg my research is all about how we view lattices that is so beautiful! <3  mathematically and metamathematically awww
This part is also going to involve a sagemath bootcamp so I can effectively carry out computations and make useful graphics

Simultaneous visibility, asymptotic density bounding, Schnirelmann density, Ergodic Theory

arXiv:2406.08197

Simultaneous visibility in the integer lattice
Daniel Berend, Rishi Kumar, Andrew Pollington
Submitted on 12 Jun 2024




Visibility on Generalized Lines of Sight

arXiv:1710.04554

Lattice point visibility on generalized lines of sight
Edray Herber Goins, Pamela E. Harris, Bethany Kubik, Aba Mbirika
Submitted on 12 Oct 2017



In this paper, we study the Dirichlet series that enumerates proper equivalence classes of full-rank sublattices of a given quadratic lattice in a hyperbolic plane—that is, a nondegenerate isotropic quadratic space of dimension 2. We derive explicit formulas for the associated zeta functions and obtain a combinatorial way to compute them. Their analytic properties lead to the intriguing consequence that a large proportion of proper classes are one-lattice classes.
Dirichlet series —> proper equivalnce classes of full rank sublattices of a quadratic lattice in hyperbolic plane.

arXiv:2505.00484

Zeta functions of quadratic lattices of a hyperbolic plane
Daejun Kim, Seok Hyeong Lee, Seungjai Lee
Submitted on 1 May 2025

Density of visible lattice points on random walks

arXiv:2009.03609

Random walks on generalized visible lattice points
Kui Liu, Xianchang Meng
Submitted on 8 Sep 2020
I honestly dont care about this that much



2. Visibility of Unimodular Integral Lattices \Lambda\subseteq\Z^n
An m dimensional Unimodular Lattice is a lattice generated by the columns of an nxm matrix whose fundamental parallelotope has m-dimensional volume 1. This is the sqrt of the Gramian M^\top M.
2.1 Visibility of \Z^n from any point x\in\Z^n
Introduces the general idea of “visible from any point” rather than just “visible from the origin”. It’s mentioned sometimes in the literature (simultaneous visibility paper) but not very common.
Anyways, this is just a jumping off point for me to begin the notion of visibility the way I want it. I can define vocabulary.
From any vantage point in space, one can consider the visibility of objects around them in that same space.
Reprove that visibility of \Z^n is 1/\zeta(n). Prove that visibility from any other point is also 1/\zeta(n)
2.2 Visibility of \Z^m\times\{0\}^{n-m} from any point x\in\Z^n
This is the visibility of affine subspaces
For m=n-1, this is the visibility of a hyperplane (from the origin) after a translation by x. I haven’t added in the SLnZ stuff yet, that will come (unimodular!)
I DONT understand the cases m\neq n-1… but I bet I can figure them outtttt <3
2.3 Visibility of \Z^n and \Z^m\times\Z_{\geq0}^{n-m} from any point x\in\Z^m\times\{0\}^{n-m}
Dual to 2.2.
This is sort of a dual notion of visibility. Rather than thinking extrinsically, like we’re viewing a space that has been embedded into our space, we can think intrinsically. From within an embedded space, what is the visibility of the ambient space? The two cases I gave should have equal density because of symmetry? but I honestly dont know!
Related to the history of visible lattice points with Olber’s paradox! <3
2.4 Visibility of \Lambda\subseteq\Z^n generated by a matrix M=UI_{nxm}V for U\in SL_{n} \Z, V\in SL_{m}\Z from any point x\in\Z^n
A generalization of 2.2.
Here I’m defining I_{nxm} to be the column matrix of the first m elementary unit vectors e_i. You can view it as the generating matrix of the lattice \Z^m\subset\Z^n
We now move to considering any transformation by unimodular matrices of these unit vectors within their own spanned space as well within the larger ambient space \Z^n.
I know that for m=n-1 this transformation has no affect on the density! But I should iron it out a little bit for my paper with Kelly.
I suspect it’s the same for m\leq n-1 but I’m not positive…
The idea is that the density of lattices doesn’t change as you transform them by SL_n \Z, what matters is the point you view them from.
2.5 Visibility of Z^n (or any half-space?) from any point x\in \Lambda\subseteq\Z^n generated by a matrix M=UI_{nxm}V for U\in SL_{n} \Z, V\in SL_{m}\Z
Dual to 2.4. A generalization of 2.3.
2.6 Conclusion :3
Given any nxm unimodular matrix, I can describe the density of points visible from a specified point x\in\Z^n
hooray!!
This is the same as asking the density on ANY hyperplane or system of linear equations (hyperplane intersections) in any dimension with any equation. woah!
3. Visibility of Non-Unimodular Integral Lattices
As in, I’m examining lattices with generating matrices whose Gramian is an integer larger than 1
3.1 Visibility of \prod_{i=1}^n r_{i}\Z from any point x\in\Z^n
Since I don’t know what’s going on it might be best for each subcase to first consider visibility from the origin and then combine it with what I’ve learned about translations.
3.1.1 r_i\neq0
This is a space that has been scaled in certain directions. It’d be interesting to cover cases like r_i = p prime, r_i=p_{i} distinct primes.
3.1.2 r_i=0, ie \Lambda is m-dimensional with m\neq n
3.2 Visibility of \Lambda generated by ANY nxm matrix M with linearly independent columns from any point x\in\Z^n.
It is naive, but I believe that the Gramian is the only thing that should determine visibility (when x is the origin)… Oh god I just have no clue.
3.3 Dual Visibility
visibility within the intrinsic space and of the ambient, extrinsic space.
3.4 Conclusion
A full descritpion of visibility of ANY integral lattice!
And under ANY linear transformation

Shearings, rotations, isometries, reflections, translations,
Translations = viewed from ANY point.
intrinsic vs extrinsic visibility

4 Squarefree —> kth power free Integers
The density of squarefree integers is 1/zeta(2). of kth power free integers is 1/zeta(k).


what if I filter for which squares I allow and don’t allow? Like how I filtered for which primes are allowed and which aren’t!


Instead of visible (gcd is prime-free), consider kth free factor. Like what about all the points whose gcd is squarefree? thatd be interesting to consider!


5. Visibility of Integral Lattices Under Certain Operations

\Lambda_1\subset\Lambda_2
\Lambda_{1}\n\Lambda_{2}
\Lambda_1\u\Lambda_2
\Lambda_1\setminus\Lambda_2
\Lambda_1\times\Lambda_2

How do these operation behave (distribute?) with the familiar linear transformations whose effects on visibility I know how to describe?
6. Commutative Algebra and Topology
Spaces modulo out visibility equivalence?
Cosets x+\Lambda for some lattice subgroup of \Z^n?

In \Z^{n-1}\times\{0\}, this is like largest squarefree factor.

largest kth power free factor?



Identification with Toroidal spaces. Chinese remainder theorem

_finnley/old/read-me.title: Read Me
_finnley/old/read-me.content:
I began by studying the density of visible lattice points on n-dimensional hyperplanes, as well as attempts to connect it with potential analogs of n-dimensional Farey Sequences and Stern-Brocot Diagrams.
This study quickly branched out into a study of the density of visible lattice points in general lattices. My longterm goal is now an adequate treatment/description of the density of visible lattice points in any integer lattice \Lambda\simeq\mathbb{Z}^m which is an affine subspace of \mathbb{Z}^n. I also want to study all possible variations of visibility as well as the affect of linear transformations (matrices in GLnZ, GLmZ, other matrices with non unit determinants, translations by any point, reflections, rotations, etc). Furthermore, I want to study the affect of topological and algebraic operations on lattices.
Very quickly in my study of all this I became exposed to new fields of math, theorems, equations, techniques, applications, etc. It has become harder and harder to organize my thoughts, short term and long term goals, relationship of my work to the relevant literature, and all the notes I’ve made of things I really need to study/read up on. That is why I’ve made this obsidian vault, so I can properly organize and take care of my goals, my research, relevant papers, and all the new math I’m learning to be able to do this subject matter justice in my treatment of it!
Eventually, I would like this obsidian vault to serve as the basis of my senior thesis for my Yale math undergrad. Additionally, if I do it right I might be able to export all of my work here to html and eventually make a math blog page devoted to my study of this stuff. That can be complete with graphics, interactive lattice and visibility visualizers, and hyperlinks to all the great papers, texts, and MATH that I’m coming across in my exploration of lattices via their visibility.
Even in everything I come across, nobody has provided a thorough treatment of this beautiful topic that lies near the hearts of so many fields of math! I think this is really something special…
Honestly the current literature is all over the place, has anybody tried to bridge it all? I don’t think so! This could honestly be my life’s work…
let me get to work!

_finnley/old/raskin-meeting-sep-9,-2025.title: raskin meeting sep 9, 2025
_finnley/old/raskin-meeting-sep-9,-2025.content:
I spent the summer of 2024 researching the density of visible lattice on n-dimensional hyperplanes and their intersections.
If M is a full rank m\times n integral matrix I am interested in the set of visible points in the lattice spanned by M, that is \{x\in\Z^n: gcd(Mx)=1\}, specifically its asymptotic density in \Z^n. It’s not hard to show that if gcd(Mx)=1, gcd(x)=1. So in other words, I’m interested in the density of the set of integral points whose visibility is invariant under the linear transformation M.
Since any matrix admits a smith normal form, we can restrict our attention to just diagonal matrices. What’s cool is the only thing that matters are these invariant factors on the diagonal, so every lattice is identifiable with a finitely generated abelian group.
For simplicity’s sake I start with the lattice p\Z\times\Z, which probabilistically and computationally I know has a density of \frac{p}{p+1}\cdot\frac{1}{\zeta(2)}
Though I haven’t completed the proof yet, I believe I am capable of using standard sieve methods to prove the asymptotic density of any diagonal matrix.
Currently I’ve been using the following textbooks as my main sources to try and gain more familiarity with the subject:

apostols introduction to analytic number theory
hardy and wright introduction to the theory of numbers
hatchers topology of numbers
Halbertsam and Richert’s Sieve Methods

also opera de cribro



hee oh
goncharov
analytic number theory
do you have any sources you recommend?
I met with my academic advisor rick kenyon at the beginning of July and he suggested I reach out to you as well as professors Losev and Goncharov. Losev said this isn’t his speciality and I should ask someone else, Goncharov didn’t reply. I really wanted to ask you because I’ve been dying to take an actual class on number theory but there just hasn’t been one offered! So when I saw you were going to teach algebraic number theory in the spring I got quite excited.
That being said, are there any other faculty members you recommend I ask to advise me?
